meta.title: Animation & Motion Design Principles for SOTY Event Horizon
meta.author: Cleanlystudio
meta.description: Comprehensive animation database covering Disney principles, easing functions, web timing, and award-winning patterns

disney_12_principles.description: Walt Disney's 12 principles of animation, adapted for web and interactive design by Frank Thomas and Ollie Johnston (1981)

squash_and_stretch.original: Objects deform to show mass, weight, and flexibility during motion
squash_and_stretch.web_adaptation: Elastic interactions — buttons that squish on press, elements that stretch during drag, rubber-band scroll overscroll effects

implementation.css: transform: scaleX(1.1) scaleY(0.9) on mousedown, return to 1,1 with spring easing
implementation.gsap: gsap.to(el, { scaleX: 1.1, scaleY: 0.9, duration: 0.15, ease: 'power2.in', yoyo: true, repeat: 1 })
implementation.principle: Volume preservation — if it squashes wider, it must get shorter (and vice versa)

event_horizon_use: Particles deforming as they approach the event horizon (spaghettification)
UI elements stretching toward the black hole in crossing chapters
Subtle elastic response on click/tap interactions

anticipation.original: A preparatory movement before the main action (wind-up before pitch)
anticipation.web_adaptation: Hover states that subtly shift before click action. Elements that pull back before animating forward. Loading indicators that compress before expanding.

implementation.css: On hover: slight scale-down (0.98) before click triggers scale-up (1.05)
implementation.gsap: Timeline: slight pullback (0.1s) → pause (0.05s) → main action
implementation.principle: The anticipation should be 1/4 to 1/3 the duration of the main action

event_horizon_use: Text elements pulling slightly toward the black hole before their reveal animation
Sound dipping before crescendo at chapter transitions
Brief darkness (anticipation) before a bright visual reveal

staging.original: Present an idea so that it is unmistakably clear — direct the audience's attention
staging.web_adaptation: Only one important action should happen at a time. Dim non-essential elements during key moments. Use motion to direct focus.

implementation.technique: Reduce opacity/blur of background elements when presenting new content
implementation.gsap: Use timeline sequencing — don't overlap competing animations
implementation.principle: If two things animate simultaneously, neither gets attention

event_horizon_use: When narrative text appears, reduce particle intensity to stage the text
During chapter transitions, clear the visual field before new chapter staging
One reveal at a time — black hole, then title, then description (not all at once)

straight_ahead_vs_pose_to_pose.original: Straight ahead: draw frame by frame from start. Pose to pose: draw key poses, then fill in between.
straight_ahead_vs_pose_to_pose.web_adaptation: Procedural animations (physics, particles) are 'straight ahead'. Keyframed/tweened animations are 'pose to pose'. Best results combine both.

implementation.straight_ahead: Particle systems, physics simulations, noise-based motion, procedural shader animations
implementation.pose_to_pose: GSAP timelines, CSS keyframes, scroll-driven interpolation
implementation.hybrid: Use keyframed timelines for high-level structure; procedural noise for organic detail within

event_horizon_use: Black hole particles: straight ahead (GPGPU physics simulation)
Text reveals: pose to pose (GSAP timeline)
Accretion disk: hybrid (orbital physics + keyframed color/intensity changes)

follow_through_and_overlapping.original: Not everything stops at the same time. Parts with different mass continue moving after the main body stops.
follow_through_and_overlapping.web_adaptation: After main element settles, secondary elements catch up with slight delay. Stagger endings. Elements with different visual weights have different deceleration rates.

implementation.gsap: Use stagger with a slight overshoot: gsap.to('.items', { y: 0, stagger: 0.05, ease: 'back.out(1.4)' })
implementation.principle: Light elements continue longer than heavy elements (inertia)
implementation.timing: Main action: 300ms. Follow-through: +100-200ms with lighter easing

event_horizon_use: When scrolling stops, particles continue drifting slightly before settling
Text that slides in: title arrives first, then subtitle follows through
Camera movement stops but lens flare/glow continues for a beat

slow_in_slow_out.original: Objects accelerate from rest and decelerate to rest — more frames at start and end, fewer in middle
slow_in_slow_out.web_adaptation: Easing functions. Nothing in the real world moves at constant speed. Every animation needs appropriate easing.

implementation.principle: ease-out for entering elements (arrive and settle). ease-in for exiting (accelerate away). ease-in-out for transitions between states.
implementation.never: NEVER use linear easing for UI animations (exception: continuous rotation, progress bars)

event_horizon_use: Every scroll-driven animation should have easing (scrub with smoothing)
Particle orbits: ease at aphelion (slow), speed at perihelion (fast) — Kepler's second law
Text opacity fades: ease-out in, ease-in out

arcs.original: Natural motion follows curved paths, not straight lines
arcs.web_adaptation: Animate along curves, not just X/Y axes. Circular and elliptical paths for orbiting elements. Even 'drop-in' animations benefit from a slight arc.

implementation.gsap_motion_path: gsap.to(el, { motionPath: { path: [{x:100, y:-50}, {x:200, y:0}], curviness: 1.5 } })
implementation.css: Combine translateX and translateY with different timing functions to create arc
implementation.principle: Straight-line motion feels robotic. Even a 5-10px vertical deviation creates organic feel.

event_horizon_use: All orbital particle paths are arcs (elliptical orbits)
Gravitationally lensed light follows geodesic curves around the black hole
Text reveal animation: characters arc in from below-and-left, not straight up

secondary_action.original: Additional animations that support the primary action without distracting from it
secondary_action.web_adaptation: Subtle background animation while primary animation plays. Particle effects accompanying a state change. Glow/shadow changes accompanying motion.

implementation.principle: Secondary actions must be subordinate — if you notice them more than the primary, they're too strong
implementation.timing: Start secondary slightly after primary (50-100ms delay) so primary registers first
implementation.examples: Button click (primary) + ripple effect (secondary). Text appear (primary) + background brightens (secondary).

event_horizon_use: Chapter title appears (primary) while accretion disk pulses (secondary)
Scroll progresses (primary) while ambient particles shift density (secondary)
Black hole rotates (primary) while lens flare subtly breathes (secondary)

timing.original: The speed of action gives meaning — fast = urgent/light, slow = heavy/important
timing.web_adaptation: Animation duration communicates the weight and importance of the change. Small changes = fast. Large changes = slower. Emotional moments = slowest.

implementation.standard_transitions: 200-400ms — panel open/close, tab switch, content change
implementation.dramatic_reveals: 800-2000ms — hero animations, chapter titles, emotional moments
implementation.ambient_loops: 2000-10000ms — breathing effects, orbital periods, atmospheric cycles

event_horizon_use: Fast: HUD data updates (100ms), particle spawns (instant)
Medium: Text reveals (600-1000ms), scroll-driven transitions (continuous)
Slow: Chapter transitions (1500-3000ms), emotional crescendos (2000-5000ms)
Very slow: Background star drift (continuous), accretion disk rotation (8-20s period)

exaggeration.original: Push animation beyond realistic to emphasize the essence of the action
exaggeration.web_adaptation: Slightly overshoot target values. Exaggerate scale changes. Push color transitions beyond subtle. Make important changes obvious.

implementation.position: Instead of stopping exactly at target, overshoot by 5-10px then settle
implementation.timing: Make fast things faster and slow things slower than realistic

event_horizon_use: Gravitational lensing effect stronger than physically accurate (more dramatic)
Time dilation effect exaggerated (clocks visually melting, not just slowing)
Spaghettification shown at a scale the eye can appreciate
Sound design: bass drops deeper than realistic, silence more absolute

solid_drawing.original: Maintain consistent three-dimensional form and volume through motion
solid_drawing.web_adaptation: 3D transforms should maintain consistent perspective. Objects rotating in 3D should feel solid, not flat. Lighting should be consistent across the scene.

implementation.three_js: Consistent camera FOV and projection. Proper depth sorting.
implementation.principle: If an element has a 3D transform, it should behave consistently in 3D space — not sometimes flat, sometimes dimensional.

event_horizon_use: The black hole and accretion disk must maintain consistent 3D geometry through all scroll positions
Particle sizes must be consistent with their 3D position (no size popping)
Gravitational lensing must be geometrically consistent (real ray-tracing, not faked)

appeal.original: Characters and scenes should have a pleasing design that engages the audience
appeal.web_adaptation: The overall aesthetic should be captivating from the first frame. Clean, intentional design. No accidental visual elements. Every pixel contributes to the whole.

implementation.principle: Appeal ≠ beauty. Appeal = engaging + clear + intentional. A terrifying black hole can be appealing if it's well-designed.
checklist: Is the animation smooth and polished?
Does it feel like someone CARED about every detail?

event_horizon_use: The black hole itself must be mesmerizing — the kind of thing you stare at
Color palette must be cohesive and intentional
Smooth 60fps performance is a prerequisite for appeal — jank destroys it
Audio and visual must harmonize — mismatched audio kills appeal instantly

easing_functions.description: Mathematical functions that control the rate of change in animations

linear.never_for: UI transitions, element movement, opacity changes

ease.character: CSS default. Quick start, gentle end. Acceptable but generic.
ease.note: Better than linear but not as polished as custom easings

ease_in.character: Slow start, fast end. Accelerating out of view.

ease_out.character: Fast start, slow end. Decelerating into position.

ease_in_out.character: Slow start, fast middle, slow end. Symmetric.

snappy.character: Fast departure, smooth arrival. Feels responsive and modern.

gentle.character: Smooth throughout. Material Design standard easing.

bouncy.character: Overshoots target then settles. Playful energy.

heavy.character: Slow acceleration and deceleration. Feels weighty.

dramatic_enter.character: Explosive start, graceful landing. High-impact entrance.

gravitational.character: Accelerating — like falling into gravity. Does not decelerate.
use_cases: Elements being pulled toward the black hole

power_easings.name: power0 (linear)
power_easings.description: No easing. Use: progress bars, continuous rotation.

power_easings.name: power2 (cubic)
power_easings.description: Standard easing. Good default for most animations.

back.description: Overshoots the target then returns. The strength parameter controls overshoot amount.

variants.back.out(1.7): Standard overshoot on arrival — playful entrance

elastic.description: Spring-like oscillation that settles. Parameters: amplitude, period.
elastic.use_cases: Attention-grabbing reveals, playful interactions, notification pop-ins

bounce.description: Simulates a ball bouncing and settling
bounce.use_cases: Rarely used in premium design — can feel cheap. Use elastic instead for sophistication.

variants.expo.out: Near-instant start, very slow end — feels like slamming into place
variants.expo.in: Very slow start, explosive end — like a spring release

expo.use_cases: Full-page transitions, dramatic camera movements, black hole approach acceleration

circ.description: Circular easing — based on quarter-circle curve. Sharp but smooth.
circ.use_cases: Circular/radial animations, orbital paths that need easing

sine.description: Sinusoidal easing — the gentlest of all. Very subtle.
sine.use_cases: Ambient breathing effects, gentle floating, atmospheric oscillation
sine.event_horizon_use: Particle field breathing, star twinkle, ambient glow pulsing

custom_ease.description: GSAP CustomEase plugin allows SVG path-based easing curves
custom_ease.use_cases: When none of the built-in easings match the desired feel

spring_physics.description: Physics-based spring animation — more natural than cubic-bezier because it models real physical behavior

stiffness.description: How tight the spring is. Higher = faster, more snappy.

damping.description: How quickly oscillation decays. Higher = less bounce.

mass.description: Weight of the animated element. Higher = slower, more momentum.

velocity.description: Initial velocity. Can carry momentum from previous animation.

frameworks.gsap: Does not have native spring physics — use CustomEase or inertia plugin
frameworks.css: Not supported natively — use JavaScript spring libraries

micro_interactions.rule: Micro-interactions should be felt, not watched. Under 200ms, the brain perceives them as 'instant response'.

page_transitions.rule: Exit animations should be 20-30% faster than enter animations. Users care about where they're going, not where they came from.

examples.implementation: Direct scroll mapping with lerp factor 0.1-0.3
Map scroll 0-1 to animation progress with scrub: true

scroll_animations.rule: Scroll animations must respond within 1 frame (16.67ms). Any lag between scroll and visual response breaks immersion.

loading_animations.rule: Loading animations should be calming, not anxiety-inducing. Smooth, predictable cycles. Show meaningful progress when possible.

text_reveal_timing.total_text_block_budget: Entire text block should reveal within 1500-3000ms maximum
text_reveal_timing.rule: Reading speed is ~250 words/minute = ~240ms per word. Reveal should be faster than reading speed.

timeline_structure.description: GSAP timelines are the backbone of complex animation sequences
rules: Use named timelines for major sequences (chapterTimeline, hudTimeline, etc.)
Nest timelines for modularity — a chapter timeline contains text, visual, and audio sub-timelines
Use labels for seek points: tl.addLabel('chapter2', '+=1')
Use relative positioning: '<' (same start), '<0.5' (0.5s after previous start), '+=0.5' (0.5s after previous end)
Always set defaults on the timeline: gsap.timeline({ defaults: { duration: 0.5, ease: 'power2.out' } })

basic_reveal.pattern: gsap.from(el, { y: 50, opacity: 0, scrollTrigger: { trigger: el, start: 'top 80%', end: 'top 20%', scrub: false } })
basic_reveal.description: Element animates once when scrolled into view

scrub_animation.pattern: gsap.to(el, { x: 500, scrollTrigger: { trigger: section, start: 'top top', end: 'bottom bottom', scrub: 1 } })
scrub_animation.description: Animation progress directly tied to scroll position. scrub: 1 adds 1 second of smooth catching up.

pin_and_animate.pattern: ScrollTrigger.create({ trigger: section, start: 'top top', end: '+=3000', pin: true, scrub: true, animation: tl })
pin_and_animate.description: Section sticks in viewport while scroll drives animation through it. end: '+=3000' means 3000px of scroll to complete.

snap_points.description: Scroll snaps to defined positions (chapters). Duration is the snap animation time.

performance_rules: Animate ONLY transform and opacity — these are GPU-composited and don't trigger layout/paint
Avoid animating: width, height, top, left, padding, margin, border, font-size — all trigger layout
Use will-change: transform on elements that will be animated (but remove after animation if static)
Batch animations in the same GSAP timeline — GSAP optimizes rendering for batched tweens
Use gsap.set() for initial states instead of CSS — ensures GSAP manages the values
Kill animations when elements leave viewport: ScrollTrigger handles this automatically with toggleActions
Use gsap.ticker for synchronizing with requestAnimationFrame instead of setInterval

scrollytelling.description: Long-form narrative driven by scroll, with visualizations changing at defined scroll points
scrollytelling.implementation: GSAP ScrollTrigger with pinned sections and scrub-driven animations
best_practices: Each chapter should have enough scroll distance for comfortable reading (~150-200vh per chapter)
Transition zones between chapters: 50-100vh of scroll
Never surprise the user — foreshadow visual changes with subtle cues
Allow the user to scroll backward and see everything in reverse (no one-way animations)

parallax.description: Layers move at different speeds based on scroll to create depth illusion
parallax.performance: Use transform: translateY() only. Never translate DOM layout properties.

progress_mapping.description: Map scroll position (0-1) to any visual property
applications: Black hole size: progress 0→1 maps to scale 0.1→5.0
Accretion disk color: progress 0→1 maps to hue shift
Camera zoom: progress 0→1 maps to FOV change
Audio volume: progress 0→1 maps to gain value

gpu_composited_properties.note: Layout > Paint > Composite in cost. Always prefer composite-only animations.

will_change.description: Hints to the browser to promote element to its own compositing layer
will_change.usage: will-change: transform, opacity — apply before animation starts
will_change.warning: Don't apply to too many elements (each gets its own GPU layer = VRAM usage). Remove after animation if element becomes static.

requestAnimationFrame.usage: All continuous animations (particle systems, camera updates) should run in rAF
requestAnimationFrame.gsap_integration: GSAP uses rAF internally via gsap.ticker — no need to manage separately

budget_breakdown.idle: ~2-3ms buffer — prevents frame drops during GC or other interrupts

frame_budget.monitoring: Use Chrome DevTools Performance panel. Look for frames exceeding 16.67ms. Use the 'Frames' section to identify dropped frames.

award_winning_animation_examples.technique: Scroll-driven 3D scene with physics and particle effects
award_winning_animation_examples.what_makes_it_great: Perfect synchronization between scroll and visual response. Zero lag. Every scroll pixel matters.

award_winning_animation_examples.site: Active Theory — A Journey Through Middle-earth
award_winning_animation_examples.what_makes_it_great: Chapter-based narrative with seamless transitions. Sound design synchronized with scroll position.
Products feel physical. Scroll = rotate/explode the product. Perfect performance.

award_winning_animation_examples.technique: Subtle GSAP scroll reveals with consistent easing
award_winning_animation_examples.what_makes_it_great: Restraint. Not every element animates. When something moves, it's meaningful.

award_winning_animation_examples.technique: Full WebGL game experience with scroll and interaction
award_winning_animation_examples.what_makes_it_great: Seamless blend of 2D UI and 3D world. Performance optimization allows complex scenes at 60fps.
Physics-based spring animations feel alive and responsive. Scroll velocity affects animation intensity.

sources.title: The Illusion of Life: Disney Animation
sources.author: Frank Thomas & Ollie Johnston
